Q1: Honestly, my study routine was not very organized at all. With limited resources and everything going on in my life, I mostly studied when I could grab a moment here or there. I tried to listen to lectures and audio materials during my commute or while doing chores at home. Seven hours a week doesn't sound like much, but those hours were scattered and hard to plan properly. I just made do with what time I had available.
Q2: My pattern varied a lot depending on what was happening in my life. I'm 27, so I have responsibilities beyond just being a student—bills to pay, family obligations, that kind of thing. When deadlines approached, I would try to carve out more time, but regular weekly study times were a luxury I couldn't always afford. Some weeks I barely touched anything, other weeks I crammed as much as I could.
Q3: Usually it was either a notification about something due or just knowing I needed to check if there was new content. Sometimes I logged in because I felt guilty about not being more active. The gamified tasks they introduced actually prompted me to log in more often because there were points and badges involved, which gave me small goals to chase even when I wasn't feeling motivated.
Q4: For a while, checking the LMS became part of my evening routine after I finished my other responsibilities. The gamified elements helped with that—seeing progress bars fill up or earning badges gave me little dopamine hits that kept me coming back. But I wouldn't say it ever became truly automatic for me. It still required conscious effort, especially on days when I was feeling low.
Q5: The video lectures were most useful to me because I learn best by listening. I could play them while doing other things, which helped me maximize my limited time. The discussion forums were also helpful because hearing other students' perspectives and questions often clarified things I was confused about. I watched over 170 minutes of video content, and that audio learning really stuck with me better than reading would have.
Q6: I often ignored the reading materials and some of the supplementary PDFs. With my auditory learning style, sitting and reading dense text just doesn't work well for me, especially when I'm already tired and stressed. I also minimally engaged with some of the optional quizzes because they felt like extra work when I was already struggling to keep up with the essentials.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if I could see how it directly connected to my assignments or exams. The gamified tasks helped because they made even small activities feel rewarding through points and progress tracking. If something felt like busywork with no clear purpose, I couldn't justify spending my limited hours on it. I needed to feel like I was actually learning something useful.
Q8: Optional activities definitely felt different—they felt like luxuries I couldn't afford. Required activities had to get done somehow, so I prioritized those even when I was exhausted. Optional ones, even if they seemed interesting, often got pushed aside because I was always behind on something more urgent. The gamified optional tasks were slightly more appealing because at least I got points for them.
Q9: Yes, absolutely. I downloaded audio versions of lectures and listened to them repeatedly offline. I also made voice recordings of myself summarizing key concepts and played those back. Sometimes I studied with a friend who had better notes, and we would discuss the material together. None of that showed up in the LMS, but it was real learning happening.
Q10: There were many times I was learning but the LMS wouldn't know. I would download a lecture once, then listen to it five or six times while cooking or on the bus. I had notebooks full of handwritten notes from those listening sessions. I also participated in a WhatsApp group with classmates where we discussed concepts and helped each other understand difficult topics.
Q11: All the time. I would access a video lecture once to download it or just let it play through, then I'd go back to it offline repeatedly. My internet access is decent but not unlimited, so downloading and studying offline saved data and let me study without connectivity issues. The LMS only saw that one access, not the hours of offline engagement.
Q12: There were definitely periods when everything felt harder. When I was feeling particularly sad or stressed, even logging in felt like climbing a mountain. Financial pressures and family issues sometimes consumed all my mental energy. During those times, I would go days without touching the course, then feel guilty and overwhelmed when I finally came back to see how much had piled up.
Q13: Absolutely, all of those factors affected me. Fatigue was constant because I don't just study—I have a whole life to manage with limited resources. When I was confused about material, I sometimes avoided the LMS because facing that confusion felt overwhelming. Competing responsibilities always won over coursework when there was a conflict, which is probably why my attendance and assignment completion were so low.
Q14: Yes, sometimes I would open materials and just stare at them without really absorbing anything. My mind would be elsewhere—worrying about money, or just feeling too sad to concentrate. I might scroll through a discussion forum or let a video play, but nothing was really sinking in. Those sessions felt wasted, but I was too drained to do better.
Q15: I postponed assignments constantly, even knowing they were important. The sadness and low motivation made starting anything feel impossibly hard. I would tell myself I'd do it tomorrow when I felt better, but tomorrow I often felt the same or worse. Fear of not doing well enough also made me avoid starting—if I didn't try, I couldn't fail, even though I knew that logic was flawed.
Q16: Deadlines were the main thing that got me to actually submit work. Without a deadline looming, I would keep putting things off. When deadlines approached, I would have bursts of activity, cramming and rushing to complete what I could. It wasn't healthy or effective, but it was the only way I managed to get anything submitted at all.
Q17: Feedback had a complicated effect on me. Good feedback lifted my spirits and motivated me to engage more. But when feedback was critical or when I received lower marks than I hoped, it made me feel worse and sometimes I would avoid the course for a while afterward. With my current emotional state, negative feedback hit harder than it probably should have.
Q18: The course structure with 16 online modules felt overwhelming from the start. Having so many courses meant I could never give proper attention to any single one. The gamified elements helped by breaking things into smaller, achievable goals, but the overall volume was still too much for someone in my situation. I needed more flexibility and fewer simultaneous demands.
Q19: The gamified tasks made engagement easier because they gave me small wins and visible progress. That helped on days when I had no internal motivation. What made engagement harder was the sheer amount of content and the expectation of regular attendance and assignment completion. The course seemed designed for students with more time and fewer life complications than me.
Q20: I think those students might be like me in some ways—logging in, browsing, maybe even engaging with content, but struggling to actually complete and submit work. Depression, anxiety, or life circumstances can make the final step of submission feel impossible even when you've done the learning. The LMS sees activity but can't see the internal barriers to completion.
Q21: That definitely reflects my experience. I performed reasonably well on exams despite low LMS activity because I was learning offline in ways the system couldn't track. My exam score was over 80, which shows I understood the material, but my assignment completion and attendance numbers tell a different story. The LMS data doesn't capture audio learning or offline study.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns of when people log in and what they click on, but they miss so much. They can't see if someone is actually paying attention or just has a tab open. They can't see offline study, group discussions, or mental health struggles. They show behavior but not understanding, effort, or circumstances. It's like judging a book by counting how many times someone opened it.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about and thinking about the material, even when you're not logged in. It means the concepts stay with you, you find yourself connecting them to real life, you discuss them with others. For me, engagement was listening to lectures multiple times until I really understood them, even if the LMS only saw one view. It's mental investment, not just clicks.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how hard I was fighting just to keep going. It wouldn't show the sadness that made every login feel heavy, or the financial stress that limited my study time. It wouldn't show my offline audio learning, my study group conversations, or the fact that I genuinely cared about understanding the material despite everything working against me. My Merit grade came from real effort that no log could capture.
